n060012: tn Heb “this Jordan”; the word “River” has been supplied in the translation for clarity (likewise in v. 11).
n060013: tc Heb “Cross over this Jordan, you and all these people, to the land that I am giving to them, to the children of Israel.” The final phrase, “to the children of Israel,” is probably a later scribal addition specifying the identity of “these people/them.”
n060014: tn Heb “Every place on which the sole of your foot walks, to you I have given it, as I said to Moses.” The second person pronouns in vv. 3-4 are plural, indicating that all the people are addressed here. The verbal form ‏נְתַתִּיו‎ (netattiv, “I have given it”) is probably a perfect of certitude, emphasizing the certainty of the action. Another option is to translate, “I have already assigned it.” In this case the verb would probably refer to the Lord’s decree to Abraham that he would give this land to his descendants.
n060015: tn A ‏מִדְבָּר‎ (midbar, “wilderness, desert”) refers to a dry region which is characterized by receiving less than twelve inches of rain per year. It therefore cannot support trees, yet may have sparse vegetation if it receives more than six inches of rain. If it receives less than six inches, then “desert” is an appropriate term (usually not a sandy desert). Most cases of the term are rendered as “wilderness” in NET. Here it refers to the southern border of the nation which stretches down into Sinai.
n060016: tn Heb “all the land of the Hittites.” The expression “the land of the Hittites” does not refer to Anatolia (modern Turkey), where the ancient Hittite kingdom of the second millennium b.c. was located, but rather to Syria, the “Hatti land” mentioned in inscriptions of the first millennium b.c. (see HALOT 363-64 s.v. ‏חִתִּי‎). The phrase is omitted in the LXX and may be a scribal addition.
n060017: tn Heb “the Great Sea,” the typical designation for the Mediterranean Sea.
n060018: tn Heb “From the wilderness and this Lebanon even to the great river, the River Euphrates, all the land of the Hittites, even to the great sea [at] the place where the sun sets, your territory will be.”
n060019: tn Heb “A man will not stand before you.” The second person pronouns in this verse are singular, indicating Joshua is the addressee.
n0600110: tn Heb “For you will cause these people to inherit the land that I swore to their fathers to give to them.” The pronoun “them” at the end of the verse refers to either the people or to the fathers.
n0600112: tn Heb “so you can be careful to do.” The use of the infinitive ‏לִשְׁמֹר‎ (lishmor, “to keep”) after the imperatives suggests that strength and bravery will be necessary for obedience. Another option is to take the form ‏לִשְׁמֹר‎ as a vocative lamed (‏ל‎) with imperative (see Isa 38:20 for an example of this construction), which could be translated, “Indeed, be careful!”
n0600114: tn Heb “be wise,” but the word can mean “be successful” by metonymy.
n0600116:
tn Heb “mouth.”
sn This law scroll must not leave your lips. The ancient practice of reading aloud to oneself as an aid to memorization is in view here.
n0600117: tn Heb “read it in undertones,” or “recite it quietly” (see HALOT 237 s.v. I ‏הגה‎).
n0600120: tn Heb “and be wise,” but the word can mean “be successful” by metonymy.
n0600121: tn Heb “Have I not commanded you?” The rhetorical question emphasizes the importance of the following command by reminding the listener that it is being repeated.
n0600125: tn Heb “to enter to possess the land which the Lord your God is giving to you to possess it.”
n0600126:
tn Heb “remember the word which Moses, the Lord’s servant, commanded you.”
sn This command can be found in Deut 3:18-20. In vv. 13-15 Joshua paraphrases the command, as the third person reference to Moses in v. 14 indicates.
n0600127:
tn Heb “is providing rest for you and is giving to you this land.”
sn “This land” refers to the trans-Jordanian lands allotted to these tribes.
n0600128: tn Heb “But you must cross over armed for battle before your brothers, all [you] mighty men of strength.”
n0600129: tn Heb “Then you may return to the land of your possession and possess it, that which Moses, the Lord’s servant, gave to you beyond the Jordan toward the rising of the sun.”
n0600131: tn Or “Only.” Here and in v. 18 this word qualifies what precedes (see also v. 7).
n0600132: tn Heb “any man who rebels against your mouth and does not listen to your words, to all which you command us, will be put to death.”
n0600133: tn Or “Only.” Here and in v. 17 this word qualifies what precedes (see also v. 7).
n060021: tn Heb “Joshua, son of Nun, sent from Shittim two men, spies, secretly, saying.”
n060023: tn Heb “they went and entered the house of a woman, a prostitute, and her name was Rahab, and they slept there.”
n060025: tn Heb “men have come here tonight from the sons of Israel.”
n060026: tn Heb “and the king of Jericho sent to Rahab, saying.”
n060028: tn The idiom “come to” (‏בוֹא אֶל‎, boʾ ʾel) probably has sexual connotations here, as it often does elsewhere when a man “comes to” a woman. If so, the phrase could be translated “your clients.” The instructions reflect Rahab’s perspective as to the identity of the men.
n060029: tn The words “the ones who came to your house” (Heb “who came to your house”) may be a euphemistic scribal addition designed to blur the sexual connotation of the preceding words.
n0600210: tn Heb “The woman took the two men and hid him.” The third masculine singular pronominal suffix on “hid” has to be a scribal error (see GKC §135.p).
n0600211: tn Heb “the men came to me.” See the note on this phrase in v. 3.
n0600212: tn Heb “And the gate was to be shut in the darkness and the men went out.”
n0600214: tn Another way to translate vv. 6-7 would be, “While she took them up to the roof and hid them…, the king’s men tried to find them….” Both of the main clauses have the subject prior to the predicate, perhaps indicating simultaneous action. (On the grammatical point, see R. J. Williams, Hebrew Syntax, 42, §235.) In this case Rahab moves the Israelite spies from the hiding place referred to in v. 4 to a safer and less accessible hiding place.
n0600215: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but is supplied for clarity.
n0600216: tn Heb “And the men chased after them [on] the road [leading to] the Jordan to the fords.” The text is written from the perspective of the king’s men. As far as they were concerned, they were chasing the spies.
n0600217: tn Heb “And they shut the gate after—as soon as the ones chasing after them went out.” The expressions “after” and “as soon as” may represent a conflation of alternate readings.
n0600219: tn Heb “she”; the referent (Rahab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0600220: tn The Hebrew text adds, “to them.” This is somewhat redundant in English and has not been translated.
n0600221: tn Heb “has given the land to you.” Rahab’s statement uses the Hebrew perfect, suggesting certitude.
n0600222: tn Heb “terror of you has fallen upon us.”
n0600224: tn Both of these statements are actually subordinated to “I know” in the Hebrew text, which reads, “I know that the Lord…and that terror of you…and that all the inhabitants….”
n0600225: tn Heb “and what you did to the two Amorite kings who were beyond the Jordan, Sihon and Og, how you annihilated them.”
n0600226: tn Heb “And we heard and our heart[s] melted and there remained no longer breath in a man because of you.”
n0600227:
tn Heb “Now, swear to me by the Lord.”
sn To swear an oath in the Lord’s name would make the Lord the witness and guarantor of the promise attached to the oath. If the person making the oath should go back on the promise, the Lord would judge him for breaking the contract.
n0600229: tn Heb “true sign,” that is, “an inviolable token or pledge.”
n0600231: tn The second person pronoun is masculine plural, indicating that Rahab’s entire family is in view.
n0600232: tn Heb “Our lives in return for you to die.” If the lives of Rahab’s family are not spared, then the spies will pay for the broken vow with their own lives.
n0600233: tn Heb “If you do not report this matter of ours.”
n0600234: tn Heb “allegiance and faithfulness.” These virtual synonyms are joined in the translation as “unswerving allegiance” to emphasize the degree of promised loyalty.
n0600235: tn The second person pronoun is feminine singular, referring specifically to Rahab.
n0600236: tn Heb “she”; the referent (Rahab) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0600237: tc The phrase “by a rope” is omitted in the LXX. It may be a later clarifying addition. If original, the omission in the LXX is likely due to an error of homoioarcton. A scribe’s or translator’s eye could have jumped from the initial ‏ב‎ (bet) in the phrase ‏בַּחֶבֶל‎ (bakhevel, “with a rope”) to the initial ‏ב‎ on the immediately following ‏בְּעַד‎ (beʿad, “through”) and accidentally omitted the intervening letters.
n0600239: tc These explanatory statements are omitted in the LXX and probably represent a later scribal addition.
n0600241: tn Heb “so that the pursuers might not meet you.”
n0600242: tn Heb “the pursuers.” The object (“you”) is not in the Hebrew text but is implied.
n0600243: tn Heb “We are free from this oath of yours which you made us swear.” The words “unless the following conditions are met” are not in the Hebrew text, but are added for clarification.
n0600244: tn Heb “Look! We are about to enter the land.”
n0600246: tn Heb “and your father and your mother and your brothers and all the house of your father gather to yourself to the house.”
n0600247: tn Heb “Anyone who goes out from the doors of your house to the outside, his blood is on his head. We are innocent.”
n0600248: tn Heb “But anyone who is with you in the house, his blood is on our head if a hand should be on him.”
n0600249: tn Heb “and if you report this matter of ours.”
n0600250: tn Heb “According to your words, so it [will be].”
n0600251: tn Heb “she sent them away and they went.”
n0600253: tn Heb “the pursuers.” The object (“them”) is added for clarification.
n0600254: tn Heb “the ones chasing them.” This has been rendered as “their pursuers” in the translation to avoid redundancy with the preceding clause.
n0600255: tn Heb “The pursuers looked in all the way and did not find [them].”
n0600256: tn The words “the river,” though not in the Hebrew text, are added for clarification.
n0600257: tn Heb “Surely the Lord has given into our hand all the land.” The report by the spies uses the Hebrew perfect, suggesting certitude.
n060031: tn Heb “And Joshua arose early in the morning and he and the Israelites left Shittim and came to the Jordan.”
n060032: tn The words “the river,” though not in the Hebrew text, have been supplied in the translation for clarity.
n060033: sn The ark of the covenant refers to the wooden chest that symbolized God’s presence among his covenant people.
n060036:
tn Heb “But there should be a distance between you and it, about 2,000 cubits in measurement.”
sn The precise distance is uncertain, but the measurement designated ‏אַמָּה‎ (ʾammah, “cubit”) was probably equivalent to approximately 18 inches, or 45 cm) in length.
n060039: tn Or more literally, “to exalt you in the eyes of all Israel.”
n0600310: tn Heb “the edge of the waters of the Jordan.” The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied for clarity.
n0600311: tn Heb “stand in the Jordan.” Here the repetition of the word “Jordan” would be redundant according to contemporary English style, so it was not included in the translation.
n0600317: tn Heb “the waters of the Jordan, the waters descending from above, will be cut off so that they will stand in one pile.”
n0600318: tn The verb, though not in the Hebrew, is added for clarification.
n0600320:
tn Heb “and the Jordan overflows all its banks all the days of harvest.”
sn The lengthy description of the priests’ arrival at the Jordan and the parenthetical reminder that the Jordan was at flood stage delay the climax of the story and add to its dramatic buildup.
n0600321: tn Heb “the waters descending from above stood still.”
n0600322: tn Heb “they stood in one pile very far away.”
n0600323:
tn Heb “the [waters] descending… were completely cut off.”
sn The Salt Sea is an ancient name for the Dead Sea.
n0600325: tn Heb “and all Israel was crossing over on dry ground until all the nation had finished crossing the Jordan.”
n060041: tn Heb “And when all the nation had finished crossing the Jordan.”
n060043: tn Heb “that this may be”; the referent of “this” (the twelve stones) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n060044: tn Heb “in order that this might be a sign among you.”
n060046: tn Heb “how the waters descending from above stood still.”
n060047: tn Here “also” has been supplied in the translation to make it clear (as indicated by v. 20) that these are not the same stones the men took from the river bed.
n0600411: sn The ‏עֲרָבָה‎ (ʿaravah, “rift valley”) extends from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. The ‏עַרְבוֹת‎ (ʿarevot) of Jericho” refer to the parts of the Jordan Valley in the vicinity of Jericho (see HALOT 880 s.v. ‏עֲרָבָה‎). The region is characterized by gently sloping plains which descend about 450 feet over the five miles from Jericho to the Jordan. Many translation say simply the “plains of Jericho” for the portion west of the Jordan and “plains of Moab” for the eastern portion. The translation here clarifies that the plains are part of the rift valley basin.
n0600416: tn Traditionally, “the ark of the testimony,” another name for the ark of the covenant. The Hebrew term ‏עֵדוּת‎ (ʿedut, “testimony” or “witness”) here refers to the Mosaic covenant and the body of stipulations contained within it (see HALOT 791 s.v. 2).
n0600417: tn Heb “and the soles of the feet of the priests were brought up to the dry land.”
n0600418:
tn Heb “and the waters of the Jordan returned to their place and went as formerly over their banks.”
sn Verses 15-18 give a more detailed account of the priests’ crossing that had been briefly described in v. 11.
n0600419: sn The first month was the month Abib (= late March-early April in the modern calendar). The preparations for Passover also began on the tenth day of the first month (Exod 12:2-3).
n0600421: tn Heb “these,” referring specifically to the twelve stones mentioned in vv. 3-7.
n0600424: tn Heb “crossed this Jordan”; the word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied to clarify the meaning.
n0600425: tn Heb “just as the Lord your God did to the Red Sea when he dried [it] up before us while we crossed over.”
n0600428: tn Heb “know the hand of the Lord that it is strong.”
n060051: tc Another textual tradition has, “while we crossed.”
n060052: tn Heb “their heart[s] melted and there was no longer in them breath (or perhaps “spirit”) because of the sons of Israel.”
n060053: tn Heb “return, circumcise the sons of Israel a second time.” The Hebrew term ‏שׁוּב‎ (shuv, “return”) is used here in an adverbial sense to indicate the repetition of an action.
n060054:
tn Or “Gibeath Haaraloth.” This name means “Hill of the Foreskins.” Many modern translations simply give the Hebrew name, although an explanatory note giving the meaning of the name is often included.
sn The name given to the place, Hill of the Foreskins was an obvious reminder of this important event.
n060055: tn Heb “All the people who went out from Egypt, the males, all the men of war, died in the wilderness in the way when they went out from Egypt.”
n060059: tn Heb “all the nation, the men of war who went out from Egypt, who did not listen to the voice of the Lord, came to an end.”
n0600510: tn Some Hebrew mss, as well as the Syriac version, support this reading. Most ancient witnesses read “us.”
n0600511:
tn Heb “flowing with.”
sn The word picture a land rich in milk and honey depicts the land as containing many grazing areas (which would produce milk) and flowering plants (which would support the bees that produced honey).
n0600512: tn Heb “their sons he raised up in their place.”
n0600515: sn One might take the disgrace of Egypt as a reference to their uncircumcised condition (see Gen 34:14), but the generation that left Egypt was circumcised (see v. 5). It more likely refers to the disgrace they experienced in Egyptian slavery. When this new generation reached the promised land and renewed their covenantal commitment to the Lord by submitting to the rite of circumcision, the Lord’s deliverance of his people from slavery, which had begun with the plagues and the crossing of the Red Sea, reached its climax. See T. C. Butler, Joshua (WBC), 59.
n0600516: sn The name Gilgal sounds like the Hebrew verb “roll away” (‏גַּלַל‎, galal).
n0600517: sn This is the area of the rift valley basin in the vicinity of Jericho (see the note at Josh 4:13).
n0600518: tn The Hebrew text adds, “on this same day.” This is somewhat redundant in English and has not been translated.
n0600519: tn Heb “the day after, when they ate.” The present translation assumes this means the day after the Passover, though it is possible it refers to the day after they began eating the land’s produce.
n0600520: tn Heb “and the sons of Israel had no more manna.”
n0600523: tn Heb “he lifted up his eyes and looked. And look, a man was standing in front of him, and his sword was drawn in his hand.” The verb ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh, “look”) invites the reader to view the scene through Joshua’s eyes. By calling the stranger “a man,” the author reflects Joshua’s perspective. The text shortly reveals his true identity (vv. 14-15).
n0600524: tn Heb “Are you for us or for our enemies?”
n0600525: tc Heb “He said, “Neither.” An alternative reading is ‏לוֹ‎ (lo, “[He said] to him”; cf. NEB). This reading is supported by many Hebrew mss, as well as the LXX and Syriac versions. The traditional reading of the MT (‏לֹא‎, loʾ, “no, neither”) is probably the product of aural confusion (the two variant readings sound the same in Hebrew). Although followed by a number of modern translations (cf. NIV, NRSV), this reading is problematic, for the commander of the Lord’s army would hardly have declared himself neutral.
n0600526: sn The Lord’s heavenly army, like an earthly army, has a commander who leads the troops. For the phrase ‏שַׂר־צְבָא‎ (sar tsevaʾ, “army commander”) in the human sphere, see among many other references Gen 21:22, 32; 26:26; Judg 4:2, 7; 1 Sam 12:9.
n0600527: sn The commander’s appearance seems to be for Joshua’s encouragement. Joshua could now lead Israel into battle knowing that the Lord’s invisible army would ensure victory.
n0600528: tn Heb “Joshua fell on his face to the ground and bowed down.”
n060062: tn Heb “was shutting and shut up.” HALOT 743 s.v. I ‏סגר‎ paraphrases, “blocking [any way of access] and blocked [against any who would leave].”
n060063: tn Heb “there was no one going out and there was no one coming in.”
n060064: tn Heb “I have given into your hand Jericho.” The Hebrew verb ‏נָתַתִּי‎ (natatti, “I have given”) is probably best understood as a perfect of certitude, indicating the certainty of the action. The Hebrew pronominal suffix “your” is singular, being addressed to Joshua as the leader and representative of the nation. To convey to the modern reader what is about to happen and who is doing it, the translation “I am about to defeat Jericho for you” has been used.
n060065: tn Heb “and go around the city, all [you] men of war, encircling the city one time.” The Hebrew verb ‏וְסַבֹּתֶם‎ (vesabbotem, “and go around”) is plural, being addressed to the whole army.
n060067: tn Heb “and it will be at the sounding of the horn, the ram’s horn, when you hear the sound of the ram’s horn.” The text of Josh 6:5 seems to be unduly repetitive, so for the sake of English style and readability, it is best to streamline the text here. The reading in the Hebrew looks like a conflation of variant readings, with the second (“when you hear the sound of the ram’s horn”) being an interpolation that assimilates the text to verse 20 (“when the army heard the sound of the horn”). Note that the words “when you hear the sound of the ram’s horn” do not appear in the LXX of verse 5.
n060068: tn Heb “all the people will shout with a loud shout.”
n0600610: tn Heb “and the people will go up, each man straight ahead.”
n0600611: tn An alternative reading is “and they said.” In this case the subject is indefinite and the verb should be translated as passive, “[the army] was told.”
n0600617: tn Heb “do not let a word come out of your mouths.”
n0600620: tn Heb “and he made the ark of the Lord go around the city, encircling one time.”
n0600621: tn Heb “and they entered the camp and spent the night in the camp.”
n0600622: tn Heb “Joshua rose early in the morning and the priests picked up the ark of the Lord.”
n0600623: tn Heb “On the seventh day they rose early, when the dawn ascended.”
n0600624: tn Heb “and they went around the city according to this manner seven times, only on that day they went around the city seven times.”
n0600627: tn Heb “for the Lord has given to you the city.” The verbal form is a perfect, probably indicating certitude here.
n0600628:
tn Or “dedicated to the Lord.”
sn To make the city set apart for the Lord would involve annihilating all the people and animals and placing its riches in the Lord’s treasury (vv. 19, 21, 24).
n0600630: tn Heb “Only you keep [away] from what is set apart [to God] so that you might not, as you are setting [it] apart, take some of what is set apart [to God] and turn the camp of Israel into what is set apart [to destruction by God] and bring trouble on it.”
n0600632: tc Heb “and the people shouted and they blew the rams’ horns.” The initial statement (“and the people shouted”) seems premature, since the verse goes on to explain that the battle cry followed the blowing of the horns. The statement has probably been accidentally duplicated from what follows. It is omitted in the LXX.
n0600637: tn Heb “and the people went up into the city, each one straight ahead, and they captured the city.”
n0600639: tn Heb “the house of the woman, the prostitute.”
n0600640: tn Heb “and bring out from there the woman and all who belong to her as you swore on oath to her.”
n0600643: tn Heb “the treasury of the house of the Lord.” Technically the Lord did not have a “house” yet, so perhaps this refers to the tabernacle using later terminology.
n0600646: tn Or “among the Israelites”; Heb “in the midst of Israel.”
n0600648: tn Normally the Hiphil of ‏שָׁבַע‎ (shavaʿ) has a causative sense (“make [someone] take an oath”; see Josh 2:17, 20), but here (see also Josh 23:7) no object is stated or implied. If Joshua is calling divine judgment down upon the one who attempts to rebuild Jericho, then “make a solemn appeal [to God as judge]” or “pronounce a curse” would be an appropriate translation. However, the tone seems stronger. Joshua appears to be announcing the certain punishment of the violator. 1 Kgs 16:34, which records the fulfillment of Joshua’s prediction, supports this. Casting Joshua in a prophetic role, it refers to Joshua’s statement as the “word of the Lord” spoken through Joshua.
n0600650: tc The LXX omits “Jericho.” It is probably a scribal addition.
n0600651: tn The Hebrew phrase ‏אָרוּר לִפְנֵי יְהוָה‎ (ʾarur lifne yehvah, “cursed [i.e., condemned] before the Lord”) also occurs in 1 Sam 26:19.
n0600652: tn Heb “With his firstborn he will lay its foundations and with his youngest he will erect its gates.” The Hebrew verb ‏יַצִּיב‎ (yatsiv, “he will erect”) is imperfect, not jussive, suggesting Joshua’s statement is a prediction, not an imprecation.
n0600653: tn Heb “and the report about him was in all the land.” The Hebrew term ‏אֶרֶץ‎ (ʾerets, “land”) may also be translated “earth.”
n060071: tn Heb “But the sons of Israel were unfaithful with unfaithfulness concerning what was set apart [to the Lord].”
n060072: tn 1 Chr 2:6 lists a “Zimri” (but no Zabdi) as one of the five sons of Zerah (cf. also Josh 7:17, 18).
n060073: tn Heb “took from what was set apart [to the Lord].”
n060074:
tn Heb “the anger of the Lord burned against the sons of Israel.”
sn This incident illustrates well the principle of corporate solidarity and corporate guilt. The sin of one man brought the Lord’s anger down upon the entire nation.
n060077: tn Heb “and they returned to Joshua and said to him.”
n060078: tn Heb “Don’t let all the people go up.”
n060079: tn Heb “Let about two thousand men or about three thousand men go up to defeat Ai.”
n0600710: tn Heb “all the people for they are small.”
n0600711: tn The meaning and correct translation of the Hebrew word ‏שְׁבָרִים‎ (shevarim) is uncertain. The translation “fissures” is based on usage of the plural form of the noun in Ps 60:4 HT (60:2 ET), where it appears to refer to cracks in the earth caused by an earthquake. Perhaps deep ravines or gorges are in view, or the word is a proper noun (“all the way to Shebarim”).
n0600712: sn The precise geographical location of the Israelite defeat at this “steep slope” is uncertain.
n0600714: tn Heb “and the heart of the people melted and became water.”
n0600715: sn Tearing one’s clothes was an outward expression of extreme sorrow (see Gen 37:34; 44:13).
n0600717: tn Heb “and fell on his face to the ground before the ark of the Lord until evening, he and the elders of Israel.”
n0600718: sn Throwing dirt on one’s head was an outward expression of extreme sorrow (see Lam 2:10; Ezek 27:30).
n0600722: tn Heb “What will you do for your great name?”
n0600724: tn Heb “Why are you falling on your face?”
n0600725: tn Heb “They have violated my covenant which I commanded them.”
n0600727: tn Heb “and also they have stolen, and also they have lied, and also they have placed [them] among their items.”
n0600728: tn Heb “they turn [the] back before their enemies because they are set apart [to destruction by the Lord].”
n0600729: tn The second person pronoun is plural in Hebrew, indicating these words are addressed to the entire nation.
n0600730: tn Heb “what is set apart [to destruction by the Lord] from your midst.”
n0600731: tn Heb “what is set apart [to destruction by the Lord] [is] in your midst.”
n0600732: tn Heb “remove what is set apart [i.e., to destruction by the Lord] from your midst.”
n0600737: tn Heb “with what was set apart [to the Lord].”
n0600740: tn See the note on “Zabdi” in Jos 7:1.
n0600741: tn Heb “and he selected Zabdi.” The Lord is the apparent subject. The LXX supports reading a passive (Niphal) form here, as does the immediate context.
n0600742: tn Heb “his”; the referent (Zabdi) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0600745: tn Heb “like this and like this I did.”
n0600746: tn Heb “Shinar,” a reference to Babylon (cf. Gen 10:10; 11:2; 14:1). Many modern translations retain the Hebrew name “Shinar” (cf. NEB, NRSV) but some use the more familiar “Babylon” (cf. NIV, NLT).
n0600748: tn Heb “Look, [it was] hidden in his tent, and the silver was beneath it.”
n0600749: tn Heb “poured out,” probably referring to the way the silver pieces poured out of their container.
n0600750: tn Or “Trouble” The name is “Achor” in Hebrew, which means “disaster” or “trouble” (also in v. 26).
n0600751: tn Or “trouble on.” The word is “achor” in Hebrew (also in the following clause).
n0600752: tc Heb “and they burned them with fire and they stoned them with stones.” These words are somewhat parenthetical in nature and are omitted in the LXX; they may represent a later scribal addition.
n0600753: tc Heb “to this day.” The phrase “to this day” is omitted in the LXX and may represent a later scribal addition.
n060082: tn Heb “Take with you all the people of war and arise, go up against Ai!”
n060083: tn Heb “I have given into our hand.” The verbal form, a perfect, is probably best understood as a perfect of certitude, indicating the certainty of the action.
n060085: tn “And Joshua and all the people of war arose to go up [against] Ai.”
n0600814: tn Heb “and they stayed between Bethel and Ai, west of Ai.”
n0600820: tn Heb “them” (referring to “the people” in the previous clause, which requires a plural pronoun). Since the translation used “army” in the previous clause, a singular pronoun (“it”) is required in English.
n0600821: tn Heb “All the people of war who were with him went up and approached and came opposite the city.”
n0600822: tn Heb “and the valley [was] between them and Ai.”
n0600824: tn Some Hebrew mss read, “spent the night in.”
n0600825: sn This probably refers to the hill country at the edge of the rift valley between Ai and Jericho. This part of the battle was probably engaged where Israel would have come up to the hill country out of the rift valley from Jericho, an ascent of about 4000 feet (with ups and downs) over ten miles.
n0600826: tn Heb “did not know that an ambush for him was behind the city.”
n0600830: tc The LXX omits the words “or Bethel.”
n0600831: tn Heb “who did not go out after Israel.”
n0600832: tn Traditionally “spear,” but see HALOT 472 s.v. ‏כִּידוֹן‎, which argues based upon evidence from the Dead Sea Scrolls that this term refers to a curved sword of some type; note the definition “scimitar” given there.
n0600833: tn Heb “it”; the referent (the city of Ai) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0600835: tn Heb “and they saw, and look.” The Hebrew term ‏הִנֵּה‎ (hinneh, “look”) draws attention to the scene and invites the audience to view the events from the perspective of the men of Ai.
n0600836: tn Heb “and there was not in them hands to flee here or there.” The Hebrew term ‏יָדַיִם‎ (yadayim, “hands”) is idiomatic for “strength.”
n0600837: tn Heb “and that the smoke of the city ascended.”
n0600838: tn Heb “and these went out from the city to meet them and they were for Israel in the middle, some on this side, and others on the other side.”
n0600840: tn Heb “in the field, in the wilderness in which they chased them.”
n0600841: tc Heb “and all of them fell by the edge of the sword until they were destroyed.” The LXX omits the words, “and all of them fell by the edge of the sword.” They may represent a later scribal addition.
n0600843: tn Heb “Joshua did not draw back his hand which held out the curved sword until he had annihilated all the residents of Ai.”
n0600845: tn Heb “and made it a permanent mound, a desolation, to this day.”
n0600846: tn Heb “on a tree until evening.” The words “leaving him exposed” are supplied in the translation for clarity.
n0600847: sn For the legal background of this action, see Deut 21:22-23.
n0600849: tn Heb “as it is written in the scroll of the law of Moses, an altar of whole stones on which no one had wielded iron.” The expression “whole stones” refers to stones in their natural condition, i.e., not carved or shaped artificially with tools (“wielded iron”).
n0600851: tn Heb “and he wrote there on the stones a duplicate of the law of Moses which he wrote before the sons of Israel.”
n0600854: tn Heb “like the resident foreigner, like the citizen.” The language is idiomatic, meaning that both groups were treated the same, at least in this instance.
n0600855:
tn Heb “as Moses, the Lord’s servant, commanded to bless the people, Israel, formerly.”
sn Moses’ earlier instructions are found in Deut 11:29.
n0600857: tn Heb “There was not a word from all which Moses commanded that Joshua did not read aloud.”
n060091: tn Heb “When all the kings who were beyond the Jordan heard.”
n060092: tn The foothills (‏שְׁפֵלָה‎, shephelah) are the transition region between the hill country and the coastal plains.
n060093: tn Heb “all the coast of the Great Sea.” The “Great Sea” was the typical designation for the Mediterranean Sea.
n060095: tn Heb “they gathered together to fight against Joshua and Israel [with] one mouth.”
n060097: tc Heb “and they went and [?].” The root and meaning of the verb form ‏יִצְטַיָּרוּ‎ (yitstayyaru) are uncertain. The Hebrew text form most likely should be ‏יִצְטַיָּדוּ‎ (yitstayyadu), read by some Hebrew mss and ancient versions, from the root ‏צוּד‎ (tsud, “take provisions,” BDB 845 s.v. II ‏צוד‎) which also occurs in v. 11. Note NRSV “they went and prepared provisions”; cf. NEB “They went and disguised themselves”; NIV “they went as a delegation.”
n0600914: tn Heb “the report about him, all that he did in Egypt.”
n0600916: tn Heb “in the day we went out to come to you.”
n0600918: tn Heb “took.” This probably means they tasted some of the food to make sure it was stale.
n0600919: tn Heb “but they did not ask the mouth of the Lord.” This refers to seeking the Lord’s will and guidance through an oracle.
n0600921: tn Heb “Joshua made peace with them and made a treaty with them to let them live, and the leaders of the community swore an oath to them.”
n0600922: tn Heb “At the end of three days, after they made the treaty with them, they heard that they were neighbors to them and in their midst they were living.”
n0600927: tn Heb “This is what we will do to them, keeping them alive so there will not be upon us anger concerning the oath which we swore to them.”
n0600928: tc Heb “and the leaders said to them.” The LXX omits the words “and the leaders said to them.”
n0600929: tn The vav (‏ו‎) consecutive construction in the Hebrew text suggests that the narrative resumes at this point. The LXX reads here, “and they will be,” understanding what follows to be a continuation of the leaders’ words rather than a comment by the narrator.
n0600931: sn Verses 22-27 appear to elaborate on v. 21b.
n0600935: tn Heb “Now you are cursed and a servant will not be cut off from you, woodcutters and water carriers for the house of my God.”
n0600938: tn Heb “so now, look, we are in your hand.”
n0600939: tn Heb “according to what is good and according to what is upright in your eyes to do us, do.”
n0600940: tn Heb “And he did to them so and he rescued them from the hand of the sons of Israel and they did not kill them.”
n0600941: tn Heb “and Joshua made them in that day woodcutters and water carriers for the community, and for the altar of the Lord to this day at the place which he chooses.”
n060103: tn Heb “as he had done to Jericho and to its king, so he did to Ai and to its king.”
n060105: tn This statement is subordinated to v. 1 in the Hebrew text, which reads literally, “When Adoni-Zedek…they feared greatly.” The subject of the plural verb at the beginning of v. 2 is probably the residents of Jerusalem.
n060106: tn Heb “Come up to me and help me.”
n060107: tn Heb “and they camped against Gibeon and fought against it.”
n060108: tn Heb “do not let your hand drop from us.”
n0601011: tn Heb “And Joshua went up from Gilgal, he and all the people of war with him, and all the brave warriors.”
n0601012: tn Heb “I have given them into your hand.” The verbal form is a perfect of certitude, emphasizing the certainty of the action.
n0601013: tn Heb “and not a man [or “one”] of them will stand before you.”
n0601014: tn Heb “Joshua came upon them suddenly, all the night he went up from Gilgal.”
n0601016: tn Heb “he.” The referent is probably Israel (mentioned at the end of the previous sentence in the verse; cf. NIV, NRSV), but it is also possible that the Lord should be understood as the referent (cf. NASB “and He slew them with a great slaughter at Gibeon”), or even Joshua (cf. NEB “and Joshua defeated them utterly in Gibeon”).
n0601017: tn Heb “struck them down with a great striking down.”
n0601020: tn Or “heaven” (also in v. 13). The Hebrew term ‏שָׁמַיִם‎ (shamayim) may be translated “heaven(s)” or “sky” depending on the context.
n0601021: tn Heb “Then Joshua spoke to the Lord in the day the Lord placed the Amorites before the sons of Israel and he said in the eyes of Israel.” It is uncertain whether the phrase “before the sons of Israel” modifies the verb “placed” (as in the present translation, “delivered the Amorites over to the Israelites”) or the verb “spoke” (“Joshua spoke to the Lord before the sons of Israel in the day the Lord delivered over the Amorites”).
n0601022:
tn Heb “Is it not written down in the Scroll of the Upright One?” Many modern translations render this as “the Book of Jashar.” Yashar (‏יָשָׁר‎) means “Upright One.”
sn The Scroll of the Upright One was apparently an ancient Israelite collection of songs and prayers (see also 2 Sam 1:18), but it has not been preserved.
n0601023:
tn Heb “and did not hurry to set [for] about a full day.”
sn The nature of the event described here is debated. Various options have been suggested, including (1) the earth stopped rotating, (2) the light of the sun somehow shone longer than normal, (3) the light of the sun was blocked by an eclipse, (4) the position of the sun and moon in the sky was interpreted as an omen, or (5) the language is figurative and metaphorical, describing the battle in poetic terms. For a good discussion of these options, see D. M. Howard, Jr., Joshua (NAC), 241-49.
n0601025: tn Heb “and appoint by it men to guard them.”
n0601026: tn Heb “But [as for] you, don’t stand still, chase after your enemies and attack them from the rear.”
n0601028: tn Heb “has given them into your hand.” The verbal form is a perfect of certitude, emphasizing the certainty of the action.
n0601029: tn Heb “When Joshua and the sons of Israel finished defeating them with a very great defeat until they were destroyed (now the survivors escaped to the fortified cities).” In the Hebrew text the initial temporal clause (“when Joshua…finished”) is subordinated to v. 21 (“the whole army returned”).
n0601030: tn Heb “all the people returned to the camp, to Joshua [at] Makkedah [in] peace.”
n0601031: tc Heb “No man.” The lamed (‏ל‎) prefixed to ‏אִישׁ‎ (ʾish, “man”) is probably dittographic (note the immediately preceding ‏יִשְׂרָאֵל‎ [yisraʾel] which ends in lamed, ‏ל‎); cf. the LXX.
n0601032: tn Heb “no man sharpened [or perhaps, “pointed”] his tongue against the sons of Israel.” Cf. NEB “not a man of the Israelites suffered so much as a scratch on his tongue,” which understands “sharpened” as “scratched” (referring to a minor wound). Most modern translations understand the Hebrew expression “sharpened his tongue” figuratively for opposition or threats against the Israelites.
n0601037: tn Heb “Joshua.” The translation has replaced the proper name with the pronoun (“he”) because a repetition of the proper name here would be redundant according to English style.
n0601042: sn For the legal background of the removal of the corpses before sundown, see Deut 21:22-23.
n0601043: tn Heb “to this very day.” The words “They remain” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0601045: tn Heb “Libnah.” Repetition of the proper name here would be redundant according to English style, so the pronoun (“it”) has been employed in the translation.
n0601046: tn Heb “he”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army).
n0601047: tn Heb “he”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army). So also for “They did to its king” and “they had done.”
n0601050: tn Heb “he”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army).
n0601053: tn Heb “he”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army). So also for “they had done to Lachish.”
n0601054: tn Heb “he”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army). So also for “they had done” and “they annihilated.”
n0601055: tn Heb “He”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army).
n0601056: tn Heb “he”; the implied subject may be Israel, or Joshua (as the commanding general of the army). So also for “They did to Debir” and “they had done to Libnah.”
n0601057: tn Heb “as he did to Hebron, so he did to Debir and its king, and as he did to Libnah and its king.” The clauses have been rearranged in the translation for stylistic reasons.
n0601058: sn The Negev is an area of central southern Judah, south of the hill country and west of the rift valley. As a geographic feature it is a depression extending south to the Gulf of Aqabah, but the biblical reference is probably to the northern portion of the region.
n0601059: sn The foothills (‏שְׁפֵלָה‎, shephelah) are the transition region from the hill country to the Mediterranean coastal plains.
n0601060: sn In contrast to the foothills on the western side of the hill country, the slopes are on the eastern side leading down to the rift valley of the Dead Sea and Jordan river.
n0601061: tn Heb “and Joshua struck them down, from Kadesh Barnea even to Gaza, and all the land of Goshen, even to Gibeon.”
n060112: tn The words “about Israel’s victories” are not in the Hebrew text but have been supplied for clarity.
n060114: tn Heb “and to the kings who [are] from the north in.”
n060115:
tn Heb “Chinneroth,” a city and plain located in the territory of Naphtali in Galilee (BDB 490 s.v. ‏כִּנֶּרֶת‎, ‏כִּנֲרוֹת‎).
sn Kinnereth was a city in Galilee located near the Sea of Galilee (Deut 3:17). The surrounding region also became known by this name (1 Kgs 15:20; cf. Matt 14:34), and eventually even the lake itself (Josh 12:3; cf. Luke 5:1). The “rift valley south of” Galilee probably refers to the northern part of the Jordan Valley from the lake to where the Jezreel Valley joins the rift valley. Dor is nearly due west from that point.
n060116: tn The verb “came” is supplied in the translation (see v. 4).
n060118: tn Heb “They and all their camps with them came out, a people as numerous as the sand which is on the edge of the sea in multitude, and [with] horses and chariots very numerous.”
n0601110: tn Heb “burn with fire”; the words “with fire” are redundant in English and have not been included in the translation.
n0601111: tn Heb “Joshua and all the people of war with him came upon them at the Waters of Merom suddenly and fell upon them.”
n0601113: tn The meaning of the Hebrew name “Misrephoth Maim” is perhaps “lime-kilns by the water” (see HALOT 641 s.v. ‏מִשְׂרָפוֹת‎).
n0601114: tn Heb “burned with fire”; the words “with fire” are redundant in English and have not been included in the translation.
n0601117: tn Heb “and they struck down all life which was in it with the edge of the sword, annihilating.”
n0601118: tn Heb “burned with fire”; the words “with fire” are redundant in English and have not been included in the translation.
n0601119: tn Heb “and he struck them down with the edge of the sword, he annihilated them.”
n0601122: tn Heb “but all the people they struck down with the edge of the sword until they destroyed them.”
n0601123: tn Heb “As the Lord commanded Moses his servant, so Moses commanded Joshua, and Joshua acted accordingly; he did not turn aside a thing from all which the Lord commanded Moses.”
n0601125: sn The Negev is an area south of the Judean hill country and west of the rift valley. As a geographic feature it is an arid depression extending south to the Gulf of Aqabah, but the biblical reference is probably to the northern part of this region.
n0601126: sn The foothills (‏שְׁפֵלָה‎, “shephelah”) refer to the transition region between the Judean hill country and the Mediterranean coastal plain.
n0601127: sn As a geographic feature, the rift valley (‏עֲרָבָה‎, ʿaravah) extends from Galilee to the Gulf of Aqaba. The reference here is probably to the Jordan Valley and the wider part of the rift valley below the Dead Sea.
n0601128: tn Heb “and struck them down and killed them.”
n0601131: tn The LXX omits this parenthetical note, which may represent a later scribal addition.
n0601133: tn Heb “for from the Lord it was to harden their heart[s] to meet for the battle with Israel, in order to annihilate them, so that they would receive no mercy, in order annihilate them, as the Lord commanded Moses.”
n0601134: tn Heb “went and cut off the Anakites from the hill country.”
n0601135: tn Heb “and from all the hill country of Israel.”
n0601137: tn Heb “according to all which the Lord said to Moses.” The translation assumes this refers to the promise of the land (see 1:3). Another possibility is that it refers to the Lord’s instructions, in which case the phrase could be translated, “just as the Lord had instructed Moses” (so NLT; cf. also NIV “had directed Moses”).
n0601138: tn Heb “and Joshua gave it for an inheritance to Israel according to their allotted portions by their tribes.”
n060122: tn Heb “beyond the Jordan, toward the rising of the sun.”
n060123: sn The rift valley is a geographic feature that extends from Mt. Hermon to the Gulf of Aqaba and includes the Sea of Galilee, the Jordan River, and the Dead Sea. The section described here extends from the border of Moab, the Arnon which runs into the middle of the Dead Sea, northward up the entire Jordan valley and beyond Galilee to Mt. Hermon at the border of Lebanon.
n060125: tc The MT reads here “and the middle of the valley,” but the reading “the city in the middle of valley” can be reconstructed on the basis of Josh 13:9, 16.
n060126: tn The words “his kingdom included” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n060127: sn The Sea of Kinnereth is another name for the Sea of Galilee. See the note on the word “Kinnereth” in 11:2.
n060128: sn The Salt Sea is another name for the Dead Sea.
n060129: sn The slopes of Pisgah lie east of the northern tip of the Dead Sea.
n0601210:
tn Heb “from the remnant of the Rephaites.”
sn The Rephaites were apparently an extremely tall ethnic group. See Deut 2:10-11, 20; 3:11.
n0601213: tn Heb “Joshua gave it to the tribes of Israel as a possession according to their allotted portions.”
n0601214: sn The foothills (‏שְׁפֵלָה‎, shephelah) are the transition region between the Judean hill country and the Mediterranean coastal plain. These are areas of eocene limestone with a distinct pattern of erosion, soil, and vegetation cover.
n0601215: sn The rift valley (‏עֲרָבָה‎, ʿaravah) is a geographic feature extending from Galilee to the Dead Sea to the Gulf of Aqaba. Here it probably refers to the Jordan valley and an area extending south of the Dead Sea by the Negev.
n0601216: sn The slopes (‏אֲשֵׁדוֹת‎, ʾashedot) refer to the ascent from the rift valley up to the hill country and to the flatlands (or wilderness) south of the hill country.
n0601217: sn The Negev is the area of central southern Judah, south of the hill country and west of the rift valley. As a geographic feature it is a depression extending south to the Gulf of Aqaba, but the biblical reference is probably to the northern portion of the region.
n0601218: tn The words “the land of” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n060131: tn Heb “was old, coming into the days.” This expression, referring to advancing in years, also occurs in the following clause.
n060132: tn Heb “the Shihor”; the word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied to clarify the meaning.
n060135: tn Heb “the five lords of the Philistines, the Gazaite, the Ashdodite, the Ashkelonite, the Gathite, and the Ekronite, and the Avvites.”
n060136: tn Or “from Teman.” The phrase is especially problematic if taken with what follows, as the traditional verse division suggests. For further discussion see T. C. Butler, Joshua (WBC), 146.
n060138: tc The reading “Arah” assumes a slight emendation of the Hebrew vowel pointing. The MT reads, “and a cave,” or “and Mearah” (if one understands the word as a proper noun).
n0601311: tn Or “the entrance to Hamath.” Most modern translations take the phrase “Lebo Hamath” to be a proper name, but often provide a note with the alternative, where “Hamath” is the proper name and ‏לְבוֹא‎ (levoʾ) is taken to mean “entrance to.”
n0601312: tn The meaning of the Hebrew name “Misrephoth Maim” is perhaps “lime-kilns by the water” (see HALOT 641 s.v. ‏מִשְׂרָפוֹת‎).
n0601313: tn Heb “only you, assign it by lots to Israel as an inheritance as I commanded you.”
n0601314: tn Heb “now apportion this land as an inheritance.”
n0601315: tn The MT reads “with him,” which is problematic, since the reference would be to the other half of the tribe of Manasseh (not the half mentioned in v. 7).
n0601316: tn Heb “received their inheritance, which Moses had assigned to them beyond the Jordan to the east.”
n0601317: tn The words “their territory started” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0601318: tn The words “their territory also included” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0601319:
tn Heb “from the remnant of the Rephaites.”
sn The Rephaites were apparently an extremely tall ethnic group. See Deut 2:10-11, 20; 3:11.
n0601322: tn Heb “he”; the referent (Moses) has been specified in the translation for clarity.
n0601324: tn That is, “their source of food and life.”
n0601326:
tn Or “promised” (Heb “spoke”).
sn For the background of this observation, see Deut 18:1-2.
n0601330: tn The words “it encompassed” are supplied in the translation for clarification.
n0601331: tn Heb “princes of Sihon, inhabitants of the land.”
n0601333: tn Heb “Balaam son of Beor, the omen-reader, the Israelites killed with the sword, along with their slain ones.”
n0601334: tn Heb “This is the inheritance of the sons of Reuben by their clans, the cities and their towns.”
n0601335: tn Heb “assigned to the tribe of Gad, to the sons of Gad.”
n0601336: tn Heb “and half of the land of the sons of Ammon.”
n0601338: tn The words “Their territory ran” are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied for clarification.
n0601339: tn Or “it included in the valley, Beth Haram.”
n0601340: sn The Sea of Kinnereth is another name for the Sea of Galilee. See the note on the word “Kinnereth” in 11:2.
n0601341: tn Heb “This is the inheritance of the sons of Gad by their clans, the cities and their towns.”
n0601342: tn Heb “assigned to the half-tribe of Manasseh, and it belonged to the half-tribe of Manasseh.”
n0601343: tn The words “their territory started at” are not in the Hebrew text, but have been supplied for clarification.
n0601344: sn The Hebrew name Havvoth Jair means “the tent villages of Jair.”
n0601345: tn Heb “These are [the lands] which Moses gave as an inheritance.”
n0601346: sn This is the area of rift valley basin to the north of the Dead Sea and east of the Jordan. Some translate as the “plains of Moab” (NASB, NIV, ESV) but this can give the wrong impression of the larger part of Moab above the rift valley. See the note at Num 22:1.
n0601347: tn Heb “beyond the Jordan, east of Jericho.” The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied to clarify the meaning.
n0601348: tn Heb “Moses did not assign an inheritance.” The word “land” has been supplied in the translation to clarify what the inheritance consisted of.
n0601349: tn That is, “their source of food and life.”
n0601350:
tn Or “as he promised”; Heb “as he spoke to.”
sn For the background of this observation, see Deut 18:1-2.
n060141: tn Heb “These are [the lands] which the sons of Israel received as an inheritance in the land of Canaan, which Eleazar the priest, Joshua son of Nun, and the heads of the fathers of the tribes assigned as an inheritance to the sons of Israel.”
n060142: tn Heb “By lot was their inheritance, as the Lord had commanded by Moses, to the nine tribes and the half-tribe.”
n060145: tn The Hebrew text adds, “in their midst.”
n060146: tn Heb “and they did not assign a portion to the Levites in the land, except cities [in which] to live and their pastures for their cattle and property.”
n060147: tn Heb “Just as the Lord had commanded Moses, so the sons of Israel did, and they divided up the land.”
n060148:
tn Heb “You know the word which the Lord spoke to Moses, the man of God, because of me and because of you in Kadesh Barnea.”
sn On this incident at Kadesh Barnea see Num 14:30.
n060149: tn Heb “and I brought back to him a word just as [was] in my heart.”
n0601412: tn Heb “made the heart[s] of the people melt.”
n0601413: tn Heb “I filled up after the Lord my God,” an idiomatic statement meaning that Caleb remained loyal to the Lord.
n0601416: tn Heb “will belong to you for an inheritance, and to your sons forever.”
n0601417: tn Heb “like my strength then, like my strength now, for battle and for going out and coming in.”
n0601420: tn Heb “Joshua blessed him and gave Hebron to Caleb son of Jephunneh as an inheritance.”
n0601421: tn Heb “Therefore Hebron belongs to Caleb son of Jephunneh for an inheritance to this day.”
n0601422: tn Heb “And he was the great man among the Anakites.”
n060151: tn Heb “The lot was to the tribe of the sons of Judah by their clans to the border of Edom, the wilderness of Zin toward the south, southward.”
n060152:
tn Heb “Their southern border was from the end of the Salt Sea, from the tongue that faces to the south.”
sn The Salt Sea is another name for the Dead Sea (also in v. 5).
n060154: tn Or “the Ascent of Akrabbim” (‏עַקְרַבִּים‎ [ʿaqrabbim] means “scorpions” in Hebrew).
n060155: tn Traditionally “the Brook of Egypt,” although a number of recent translations have “the Wadi of Egypt” (cf. NAB, NIV, NRSV).
n060156: tn The translation follows the LXX at this point. The MT reads, “This will be your southern border.”
n060158: tn The word “River” is not in the Hebrew text, but has been supplied for clarity.
n060159: tn Heb “the border on the northern side was from the tongue of the sea, from the end of the Jordan.”

n0602236: tn Heb “Is it not [true that] Achan son of Zerah was unfaithful with unfaithfulness concerning what was set apart [to the Lord] and against all the assembly of Israel there was anger?”
n0602237: tn The second half of the verse reads literally, “and he [was] one man, he did not die for his sin.” There are at least two possible ways to explain this statement: (1) One might interpret the statement to mean that Achan was not the only person who died for his sin. In this case it could be translated, “and he was not the only one to die because of his sin.” (2) Another option, the one reflected in the translation, is to take the words ‏וְהוּא אִישׁ אֶחָד‎ (vehuʾ ʾish ʾekhad, “and he [was] one man”) as a concessive clause and join it with what precedes. The remaining words (‏לֹא גָוַע בַּעֲוֹנוֹ‎, loʾ gavaʿ baʿavono) must then be taken as a rhetorical question (“Did he not die for his sin?”). Taking the last sentence as interrogative is consistent with the first part of the verse, a rhetorical question introduced with the interrogative particle. The present translation has converted these rhetorical questions into affirmative statements to bring out more clearly the points they are emphasizing. For further discussion, see T. C. Butler, Joshua (WBC), 240.

n0602452:
tn Heb “lift up” or “take away.”
sn This assertion obviously needs qualification, for the OT elsewhere affirms that God does forgive. Joshua is referring to the persistent national rebellion against the Mosaic covenant that eventually causes God to decree unconditionally the nation’s exile.
n0602455: tn The words “against you” are added for clarification.
n0602458: tn The translation assumes that ‏כִּי‎ (ki) is emphatic. Another option is to take it as explanatory, “No, for we will….”
n0602460: tn Heb “You are witnesses against yourselves that you have chosen for yourselves the Lord to serve him.”
n0602461: sn Like witnesses in a court of law, Israel’s solemn vow to worship the Lord will testify against them in the divine court if the nation ever violates its commitment.
n0602462: tn The words “Joshua said” are supplied for clarification.
n0602463: tn Heb “bend your heart toward.” The term ‏לֵבָב‎ (levav, “heart”) probably here refers to the people’s volition or will.
n0602468: tn Heb “all the words of the Lord which he spoke with us.”
n0602470: tn Heb “And Joshua sent the people away, each to his inheritance.”
n0602474: tn Heb “all the days of Joshua and all the days of the elders who outlived him.”
n0602475: tn Heb “who knew all the work of the Lord which he had done for Israel.”
n0602476: tn Heb “one hundred qesitahs.” The Hebrew word ‏קְשִׂיטָה‎ (qesitah) is generally understood to refer to a unit of money, but the value and/or weight is unknown. The word occurs only here and in Gen 33:19 and Job 42:11.
n0602477: tn Heb “and they became for the sons of Joseph an inheritance.” One might think “bones” is the subject of the verb “they became,” but the verb is masculine, while “bones” is feminine. The translation follows the emendation suggested in the BHS note, which appeals to the Syriac and Vulgate for support. The emended reading understands “the part (of the field)” as the subject of the verb “became.” The emended verb is feminine singular; this agrees with “the part” (of the field), which is feminine in Hebrew.
n0602478: tn Heb “in Gibeah of Phinehas, his son, which had been given to him in the hill country of Ephraim.”
